title: Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence
exclusions: Does NOT cover physical deletion (that is UC-22.36.2); focuses on stop-processing only.
evidence: Saved search 'consent_withdrawal_propagation' runs every 30 minutes and writes per-system stop-processing acks to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.7(3) requires that withdrawal of consent is as easy as giving it and must take effect without delay; the UC measures the system-by-system stop-processing latency.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.7 (Conditions for consent) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.2: Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA §1798.100 and the GPC obligations under §1798.135 require honoring opt-outs promptly; propagation monitoring is necessary evidence.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.100 (Consumer right to know) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.2: Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Derived from GDPR@2016/679 Art.7 (parent assurance='full'). UK GDPR preserves parent clause numbering; inherited assurance degraded one step.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that UK GDPR Art.7 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.2: Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a consent withdrawal is recorded but any in-scope downstream system continues to emit processing events for that subject after 24 hours, the UC fires naming the system and activity_type.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Processing activities with lawful basis ≠ consent are NOT treated as withdrawal violations.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=cmp action=withdraw earliest=-7d
| join subject_token [search index=app_activity (lawful_basis=consent) earliest=-7d]
| where _time>=withdrawal_time + 86400
| stats values(system) as systems_still_processing count by subject_token withdrawal_time
| where count>0
| table withdrawal_time subject_token systems_still_processing count
description: Cross-checks consent-withdrawal events against live processing telemetry. If a system continues to process a subject's data more than 24 hours after withdrawal, the UC raises a finding and opens a remediation task.
value: Withdrawals that do not propagate are a primary source of supervisory-authority enforcement; this UC turns a weak process control into a tested technical control.
implementation: (1) Instrument each consent-governed processing activity to emit subject_token and lawful_basis; (2) ingest withdrawals from the CMP; (3) run every 30 min; (4) on hit, open a SOAR case with the system owner. Indexes required: `index=app_activity`, `index=cmp`.
visualization: Matrix of system × withdrawal-breach count (7d), single-value 'withdrawals propagated within 24h'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `cmp`, `app_activity` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.7`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.7 (Conditions for consent) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.2: Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `cmp`, `app_activity` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Instrument each consent-governed processing activity to emit subject_token and lawful_basis. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** ingest withdrawals from the CMP. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** run every 30 min. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on hit, open a SOAR case with the system owner. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-processing evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of GDPR control activity.

This search correlates `index=cmp` (sourcetype=`the primary`) on the outer side with `index=app_activity` (sourcetype=`another`) on the inner side, joined on `subject_token` across the last 7 days. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `subject_token` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.37.2: Consent withdrawal propagation SLA — downstream stop-process]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.37.2,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=cmp sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `count`, `subject_token`, `systems_still_processing`, `withdrawal_time`. Run:

```spl
index=cmp sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table count, subject_token, systems_still_processing, withdrawal_time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `count`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(count) as p50 perc90(count) as p90 perc99(count) as p99 max(count) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.37.2: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Matrix Of System × Withdrawal-Breach Count (7D) (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Single-Value 'Withdrawals Propagated Within 24H' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you track consent and choices over time so your marketing and storage stay aligned with what people agreed to.
